text: It's weird that no one has seemed to really understand the horror of this movie. Both sides commit countless atrocities during the film. Prisoners of war are executed, bodies are piled in mass graves, citizens are tortured and hung because there are no police or courts, and noncombatants on both sides are gunned down while trying to surrender. It really goes out of its way to stay apolitical. Even the most loaded phrase of the film ("the antifa massacre") suggests two distinct political possibilities. I really think that the point of this movie is to show how horrific and nightmarish a civil war in America would be, not to mention all of the awful redounding effects of such a conflict: major civil wars often devolve into smaller and more local grievances and gripes, and people often devolve into petty animals. Pretty soon, we all might end up standing at the lip of a mass grave, wondering where the next threat will come from, seeing literal red. One thing we should all realize is that this is not a future we want, so please don't let this movie serve as an engine for your political ideology. Seeing the world with such a myopic obsession is what got us into this mess in the first place.

text:
Lately, I’ve been thinking a lot about the idea of American exceptionalism—especially the belief that Americans would somehow be uniquely resistant to authoritarianism. Films like Civil War by Alex Garland tap into that myth. They portray bold uprisings, states breaking away in defiance, people rising up en masse to fight tyranny. But honestly, I’m not convinced that’s how things would really go.

When I look at the political climate in the U.S. now, especially with the return of figures like Donald Trump who’ve shown clear authoritarian tendencies, what I see isn’t widespread resistance—it’s resignation. A lot of people just seem to be keeping their heads down, hoping things will pass. There’s a real sense of fear, weariness, and even helplessness. The courts, the media, the institutions that are supposed to hold things in check—they often seem either overwhelmed or complicit. That doesn’t lead to rebellion; it leads to quiet compliance.

And this idea that Americans are somehow fundamentally different from people in other countries? That they’ll always stand up for liberty while others cower? That just doesn’t hold water. People everywhere are people. When faced with fear, most will try to protect their families, avoid trouble, and wait things out. It’s not necessarily cowardice—it’s just a survival instinct.

Revolutions, historically, don’t always come from noble courage in the face of tyranny. They come when people have no other option, or when a critical mass sees an opening. But most of the time, especially in the early stages of authoritarianism, people try to adapt. They rationalize, they withdraw, they stay quiet.

So while Civil War might capture the emotional intensity of a fractured America, I don’t think it reflects the reality of how resistance plays out. If things keep sliding further, I suspect the reaction won’t be some dramatic uprising—but more likely a slow, uncertain acquiescence. And that’s what I find really chilling.

California is further to the left than Texas is to the right, but California also recently had a Republican governor for 8 years. 

Most Texans and Californians agree on most things - the imagined 'huge divide' is a myth, propagated by politicians (to keep people fighting amongst themselves and not looking at how political parties are failing) and the media (because there's no money to be made from no conflict).

They intentionally took the two biggest red and blue states (Texas and California) and had them align together in order to keep this movie from becoming a propaganda for either real world political party.

It’s soooo real and could be a possible outcome if things continue as that are here in the states. It has a lot of shock and in your face moments. The kind you normally only hear about happening in other countries outside the United States.

In a couple of scenes it was like taking the opening to Saving Private Ryan on the beach and multiplying the disturbing parts by a factor of x2. And I’m not talking horror movie level either. Freddy, Jason, Chucky and Michael would all run away sacred at some of the things/scenes in this movie. The things you see and the things some people do. Honestly I’d give it a higher rating than R. Definitely don’t take your kids to see it. 

This is isn’t some Arnold Schwarzenegger action flick type of movie either. I would put Civil War on the same level as the original Red Dawn movie, but then multiply that by a factor of x2.

But the powerful message is clear. This could happen here. We as a country are in danger and we need to come together as one or this could very well happen to us.

Definitely worth seeing again. This movie will stay with me for a long time.
